respStmt.name: Dirk Van Hulle
respStmt.resp: SBDMP coordinator, encoding scheme, TEI customization

respStmt.name: Vincent Neyt
respStmt.resp: Project architecture, encoding scheme, TEI customization

publicationStmt.authority: Centre for Manuscript Genetics (University of Antwerp)

availability.p: Not to be distributed outside the project

sourceDesc.p:
Abbreviated in the Manuscript Chronology as ET1
Untitled ['Krapp's Last Tape']. Typed manuscript, signed, March 1958, 4 pp., 4to. Typescript I. With numerous autograph revisions and additions in black ink. There are a few doodles. The text varies considerably from the published version and lacks, in particular, the introductory stage directions and description of the setting. The protagonist appears as 'A'. At the top of page 1 Beckett has written: 'Typescript I of Krapp's Last Tape 3.58.' Inscribed, in blue ink, at the bottom of page 4: 'for / Jake Schwartz / Sam. Beckett / March 1958'.
Lake, Carlton, ed. (1984), No Symbols Where None Intended: A Catalogue of Books, Manuscripts and Other Material Relating to Samuel Beckett in the Collections of the Humanities Research Center (Austin: Humanities Research Center), p. 105

head.add: Typescript I of Krapp's Last Tape 3.58.

div.note: This folder with four typescripts of \'Krapp\'s Last Tape\' is accompanied by a typed letter to Jake Schwartz on a half A4 sheet: "April 5th 1958 / Ussy / Dear Jake / Thank you for letter and TLS review a copy of which I received from Harold Hobson by the same post. I had not seen it. I am told it is by Anthony Cronin. He certainly does me proud. The Krapp monol text has nothing to do with Dublin. I have written it for Pat Magee and George Devine and it will be played by the former at the Royal Court with Endgame. This will be next May or in the early autumn, I do not yet know which. I might possibly come over to make a nuisance of myself at the rehearsals of Endg Krapp, I am very anxious for it to be done right. It will be published in a coming number of Evergreen Review, I shall reserve a copy for you. I hear the Yeats exhibition was an immense success. Waddington has been here buying more pictures from my friend Hayden which pleases me much. He will exhibit him later, first in a group, then I hope one man. I have been trying to work in French, with scant success. The weather is perishing, but great peace here above the valley, you wouldn\'t know it was Easter. Ever

seg.stage: (strong young voice, but unmistakeably A's. Rather pompous.)

p.seg:
Thirty-seven today, sound apparently in wind (
he he rimes sic; archaic spelling of "rhymes" this word with mind) and limb,
apart from my old trouble, and intellectually I have
little doubt at the peak of my powers, or thereabouts.

Celebrated the solemn dreadful occasion, as in recent years, quietly
at the Winehouse.
No one face there I knew.

Sat before the fire
with closed eyes separating the grain from the chaff.

Jotted
down a few notes, on the back of an envelope.

It is good to
be home again, in my old things rags.

The new light over above my desk table
is a great improvement.

With all this darkness round me I feel
am less alone.
In a way.

I like to get up
and move about in it, then back here to...(hesitates)...
myself.

The grain, what di o I mean by that, I mean...(Pause.) I
suppose I mean those things worth having when all the dust
has - when all this dust has settled.

I close my eyes and
try and imagine them.

Extraordinary silence this evening. I strain my ears and
do not hear a sound.

Old Miss Williams Beamish always sings at this
hour.

seg.add: The songs of her girlhood, she says.⁁ ← Arrow pointing towards addition in left margin.

p.seg: But not tonight.

seg.add: Shall I sing when I am her age ?
↑ Arrow to indicate supralinear addition.if I ever am.

Did I sing
as a boy?

p.seg: I have just listened to an old year. , ⁁ passages at random.

I did not check up
in the register book, but it must be at least ten or twelve or fifteen years
ago.

At that time I was living on and off with Celia Furry in
Street.

add.note: In the left-hand margin, underneath \'Hopeless business\', Beckett has written \'Insert\', followed by an insertion mark in the shape of the Greek letter lambda, corresponding to the lambda in the main text after \'Street.\'. The passage to be inserted is written on page 21r of the \'ETE 56\' notebook (MS UoR 1227-7-7-1).

seg.del:
I must say I - (A switches off machine, broods
at length, switches on again.) - find this a great help before
embarking on a new...er....review conspectus.

I find it hard It is hard to believe
that young man was I.

p.seg: Even t The voice is unfamiliar not mine. .

Aspirat
ions, .
r Resolutions.
To drink less, in particular.

s Seventeen hundred hours, out of the preceding 8 eight thousand
odd, consumed on licensed premises alone.

Plans for a fuller
less aspiring
less exhausting
sexual life.
Last illness of his father.

References to his
life's work magnum opus
↓ Arrow to indicate addition marginbottom.

Closing with a - (brief laugh) - an appeal to
a plea squeal
Providence.

What remains of all that
lumber misery?

A girl in a shabby green dress coat, on a railway-
station platform?
When I look -
p.stage:
( A si w w itches off machine, broods,
consults looks at watch, gets up and
goes backstage into shadows.
Pause of ten seconds.
Sound of
cork popping.
Ten seconds.
Second cork.
Ten seconds.
Third
cork? Te cork .
Ten seconds.
Sudden brief burst of song
(Brief burst of raucous song.)
He comes back into light from an
unexpected angle, say front right , sits down, resumes his
pose, switches on machine.)
p.seg:
- back on the year that is gone,
with the old eyes to come, there is of course the house on the
canal where mother lay dying, in the early autumn, after her long
widowhood, and the

bench by the weir from where I could see her
window.
I sat there, wishing she were gone.

Quite
a number of people I got to know then, oh I mean by appearance, nursemaids
and children, old men, dogs.

One dark young woman beauty I remember par
ticularly, ⁁
all white and starch ⁁ with a big ba lack pram.

Whenever I looked in
her direction she always had her eyes on me.

And yet But when at last
I ventured
⁁
was foolish enough to speak to her she threatened to call a policeman.

The
face she had, !
e Eyes
like...moonstones.

I was there when -o - 5 (
h He
switches off, broods, w switches on again)- the blind went down, one
of those dirty brown roller affairs, throwing a ball fo r a for a
dog as it happened
Stet.
\'Stet.\' in the left margin.
I was
.
I looked up and there it was.

I sat a
for a few
minutes moment or so with the ball in my hand and the dog barking and pawing at
me. ⁁

In the x end Then I held it out to him and he took it in his mouth, very
gently.
An old tennis ball, black and sodden, but unpunctured.

p.note: Line connecting the main text to the addition in the top margin.

add.seg: Bowels are better on the whole.

p.seg:
Intellecy tually a year of deep
discouragement
gloom
profound gloom until that wonderful night in
March, at the end of the pier, in the high wind, when suddenly I
saw the whole thing.

This, I realized over my wine, I imagine is what I have
chiefly to record set down this evening, against the day when my work will
be done and perhaps no place left in my memory, and no thankful
ness, fot r e the moment miracle that - made it possible
⁁
. - (Pause) - for the fire that set it alight.⁁

What I saw then was that
the assumt p i tion I had been going on all these years, my life, namely that -

seg.stage:
(He switches off machine impatiently, winds tape forward, switches
on again)

p.seg:
- granite rocks the foam flying up in the light of the
beacon and the anemometer spinning like a propellor, clear to me
at last that the dark I had have struggled to keep out of my work at bay
is in r reality its the true my most valuable - -

seg.stage:
(He curses, switches off, winds tape for
x ward, switches on again)

p.seg:
- strange association till my dying day
of storm and night with the light of understanding and the peace -

seg.stage:
(He curses louder, switches off, winds tape forward, switches on
again)

p.seg: - my face in her breasts and my hand on her.

We lay there
without moving.

But underneath us all moved, and moved us, up
gently, up and down, and to and fro . from side to side.
Past midnight.
Never knew such a silence.

seg.del: The coming year will decide. May I be given the strength and the -

seg.stage: He switches off, winds tape back, switches on again.

p.seg:
- upper lake, with the punt, bathed off the bank, then pushed out
into the str ream and drifted.

She lay stretched out on the floor
boards with her hands under her head and her eyes closed.

Hot Blazing sun,
with a bit of a breeze, and the water nice and lively.

I noticed
a scratch on her thigh and asked her how she got it.

Picking gos ose
berries, she said.

I said again I thought it was hopeless and no
good going on, and she agreed, without opening her eyes.

I asked
3
her to look at me and after a moment or so / Line connecting addition in top margin - pause - after a moment or so she did, but her
eyes just slits, because of the glare.

I leant bent over her to get
them in the shadow and they opened.

We drifted in among the
reeds and got stuck.

The way they went down
sank
bent, sighing, under before the blunt
stem.

I lay down across her with my face in her breats breasts and
my hand on her.
We lay without moving.

But under us all moved, and
moved us, up and down, and fr rom side to side.
Past midnight.
Never knew -

seg.hi:
fumblesi in
feels in
fumbles in his waistcoat pockets, takes out
an old enb velope, fumbles, puts back the envelope,/ Short line connecting addition in top margin. looks at his watch, gt es \'gtes\' changed to \'gets\' by means of a wavy line. up and goes
right into shadows.

Sound of bottle against glass, then
brief siphon.

p.seg:
He comes back front left into light, with an unused tape
in his hand, sits down, takes tape off machine and lays it on table,
puts other tape on machine, takes envelope from his pocket, lays it
on the table, switches on and begins to record.

Just been listening to that stupid bastard I was 30 years ago,
find it hard to believe I was ever as bad as that.

The eyes she
had!
p.stage:
( Broods, realizes he is recording silence, swith c c hes off,
broods.)
p.seg: Everything there, everything, all the -

seg.stage:
(Realizes this
is not being recorded, switches on.)

p.seg:
Everything there, every- bleed
ing thing
⁁
thing on earth,, all the light and dark of the
fxxd
vice and spirit
crystal and sorrow
and rejoicing Although there is no insertion mark after "of the" the phrase in the left margin is included at this point in the text in later versions....(hesitates)...ages.

Let
that go!
Jesus!
Take his mind off his homework!
Jesus!

Ah
well, maybe he was right.
Maybe he was right.
p.stage:
( Broods.
Switches off.
Consults envelope.
Crumples it and throws it away.
Broods.
Switches on.

p.seg: Not a thing to tell Nothing to say.
What's a year now?

Reverie and obstipation
⁁
galloping constipation. .
Seventeen copies sold, one pound six and something, eight probably.

Getting known
xx
.

Ca r r awled out once or twice, before the summer was
over.

Sat in the park in the middle of the brats and skivvies,
dreaming and wishing I were gone.
Last fancies.

Swore to fight keep them
off at bay.
Aspirations.
Resolutions.

Burn s t the eyes out of me reading
Effie again, a page a day, with tear rs again.

Could have been happy
with her, up there on the cold sea, and the pines, and the dunes.
Fanny came in a couple of times.
Fat old whore.

Couldn't
do much, but better than nothing.
The last time wasn't so bad.

How
do you do it, she said, at your age?

I told her I'd been saving up
for her all my life.

Went to vespers once, like when I
was a child first time since I was a boy left school,.
Slept.

Sometimes wondered in the night if a
last effort mightn't -

Empty the bottle now and get to
your bed.
Finish this vomit puke tomorrow?.
Or m leave it at that.
Leave it at that.

Lie propped up on my back in the dark,
and wander roam.

Be again in the dingle on a Xmas Eve, picking
holly.

Be again on Croghan on a Sunday morning with the dog
⁁, in the snow
snow, with the bitch ⁁ stop and listen to the bells.
And so on.
Be again.
All that old misery.
Once wasn't enough.
Lie down across her.
p.stage:
( Long pause.
He suddenly bends to
machine, switches off, wrenches off tape, throws it away, puts on
4
the other, winds it to the passage he wants, switches on, listens
staring front.

p.seg: - gooseberries, she said?.

I said again I thought it was hopeless
and no good going on, and she agreed, without opening her eyes.

I asked her to look at me and after a moment or so / Line connecting addition in top margin. - (Pause) - after a moment or so she did, but
her eyes just slits because of the glare.

I leant bent over her to get
them in the shadow and they opened.

We drifted in among the
reeds and got stuck.

They way they went down bent, sighing, before the
stem.

I lay down across her with my face in her breasts and my
hand on her.
We lay without moving.

But under us all moved and m
moved us, up and down, and from side to side.
Past midnight.
Never knew such a silence.
Here I end this tape . , - (hesitates) - stage.

seg.add: box four, number th box - (pause) - four, number - (pause) - three.

p.seg: Perhaps my best yea years are over past.

When
there was a chance of happiness.
But I wouldn't want them back.
Not with the fire in my me now.
No, I wouldn't have want them back.

seg.hi: A sits motionless staring before him.

p.seg: The tape runs on in silence.
Curtain centred

seg.add:
for
Jake Schwartz
Sam. Beckett
March 1958
